words.definition: difficult to understand; obscure
words.example: The philosopher's abstruse arguments baffled even his colleagues.

words.definition: belonging to a period other than the one being portrayed; outdated
words.example: The film's anachronistic costumes distracted from its historical accuracy.

words.definition: of doubtful authenticity, although widely circulated as true
words.example: The story of the falling apple is likely apocryphal.

words.definition: an abrupt transition from the elevated to the ordinary, producing an anticlimax
words.example: The tragic scene collapsed into bathos with one clumsy line.

words.definition: high-sounding but with little meaning; inflated
words.example: The senator's bombastic speech impressed no one with substance.

words.definition: excessively complicated, typically involving intrigue
words.example: Navigating the byzantine bureaucracy took months.

words.definition: the treatment of light and shade in art; a strong contrast
words.example: Caravaggio is renowned for his dramatic chiaroscuro.

words.definition: having knowledge or awareness of something
words.example: The author was cognizant of the criticism her work would receive.

words.definition: stubbornly disobedient; resistant to authority
words.example: The contumacious witness refused to answer the court's questions.

words.definition: causing harm, often in a subtle or unexpected way
words.example: Excessive screen time can have a deleterious effect on sleep.

words.definition: a political leader who exploits popular prejudices for personal gain
words.example: Historians warn against the rise of demagogues in times of crisis.

words.definition: the final resolution of the plot of a narrative
words.example: The novel's denouement ties every subplot together.

words.definition: light, delicate, and translucent
words.example: The dancer wore a diaphanous veil that caught the stage light.

words.definition: cheerful and full of energy
words.example: Her ebullient personality lit up every room she entered.

words.definition: relating to the Christian Church or its clergy
words.example: The novel is set within an ecclesiastical court of the medieval era.

words.definition: affected, overrefined, and lacking vigour
words.example: The critic dismissed the poetry as effete and self-indulgent.

words.definition: expressing sorrowful lamentation, especially for the dead
words.example: The poem's elegiac tone mourns a vanished world.

words.definition: the branch of philosophy concerned with the nature and limits of knowledge
words.example: Her thesis draws heavily on Cartesian epistemology.

words.definition: mental calmness and composure, especially in difficulty
words.example: He accepted the harsh review with remarkable equanimity.

words.definition: having or showing great knowledge or learning
words.example: The erudite professor could discuss any era of history with ease.

words.definition: extremely delicate and light in a way that seems too perfect for this world
words.example: The choir's ethereal harmonies filled the cathedral.

words.definition: the study of the origin and historical development of words
words.example: The etymology of 'quarantine' traces back to the Italian for forty days.

words.definition: the quality of being pleasing to the ear
words.example: The poet chose each word for its euphony as much as its meaning.

words.definition: relating to human existence, especially its meaning and purpose
words.example: The novel raises existential questions about free will.

words.definition: producing or capable of producing abundant results; fertile
words.example: The Renaissance was a fecund period for the arts.

words.definition: well chosen or apt; pleasingly appropriate
words.example: She found a felicitous phrase to close her speech.

words.definition: excessively talkative, especially on trivial matters
words.example: The garrulous old sailor told the same story three times.

words.definition: the theory and methodology of interpretation, especially of texts
words.example: Biblical hermeneutics has shaped centuries of theological debate.

words.definition: excessive pride or self-confidence, often leading to downfall
words.example: The tragic hero's hubris ultimately causes his ruin.

words.definition: the rejection or destruction of cherished beliefs and institutions
words.example: The movement's iconoclasm shocked the art establishment.

words.definition: unable to be resisted or avoided; inescapable
words.example: Old age is an ineluctable fact of life.

words.definition: just begun and not fully formed; rudimentary
words.example: Her inchoate plan needed far more detail before it could work.

words.definition: not able to be reformed or corrected
words.example: The incorrigible prankster was expelled after his fifth offence.

words.definition: too great or extreme to be expressed in words
words.example: She felt an ineffable joy watching the sunrise from the summit.

words.definition: showing a casual lack of concern; carefree
words.example: His insouciant attitude towards deadlines infuriated his editor.

words.definition: unwilling to change one's views or agree about something
words.example: The two sides remained intransigent throughout the negotiations.

words.definition: harsh, abusive language directed against someone
words.example: The pamphlet was full of invective against the ruling class.

words.definition: using very few words; terse
words.example: His laconic reply gave nothing away.

words.definition: looking or sounding sad and dismal
words.example: The lugubrious music set the tone for the film's final act.

words.definition: a plot or scheme, typically involving underhand means
words.example: The play centres on the machinations of a scheming courtier.

words.definition: generous or forgiving, especially toward a rival
words.example: The champion was magnanimous in victory, praising her opponent.

words.definition: sweet or musical; pleasant to hear
words.example: His mellifluous voice made even the shipping forecast sound beautiful.

words.definition: not telling the truth; lying
words.example: The report exposed the minister's mendacious claims.

words.definition: a figure of speech in which the name of an attribute is substituted for the thing itself
words.example: 'The crown' is a metonymy for the monarchy.

words.definition: imitation or representation of reality in art and literature
words.example: Aristotle considered mimesis central to the purpose of tragedy.

words.definition: a person who dislikes humankind and avoids human society
words.example: The old misanthrope lived alone at the edge of the village.

words.definition: vague, unclear, or ill-defined
words.example: The proposal remained nebulous even after the meeting.

words.definition: to deliberately make something unclear or confusing
words.example: The report seemed designed to obfuscate the true costs.

words.definition: obedient or attentive to an excessive, servile degree
words.example: The obsequious waiter fussed over every tiny detail.

words.definition: the branch of philosophy concerned with the nature of being and existence
words.example: The seminar examined questions of ontology raised by artificial intelligence.

words.definition: harsh public criticism or disgrace
words.example: The scandal brought widespread opprobrium upon the company.

words.definition: designed to impress or attract notice; showy
words.example: The billionaire's ostentatious mansion dominated the coastline.

words.definition: a public speech or text in praise of someone
words.example: The eulogy became a panegyric to the professor's decades of teaching.

words.definition: travelling from place to place, especially for work
words.example: The peripatetic scholar lectured at a dozen universities each year.

words.definition: having keen insight or understanding; shrewd
words.example: Her perspicacious analysis exposed the flaw in the argument.

words.definition: holding firmly to an opinion or course of action; stubbornly persistent
words.example: His pertinacious pursuit of the truth eventually solved the case.

words.definition: a person who is hostile or indifferent to culture and the arts
words.example: He was mocked as a philistine for never having read a novel.

words.definition: relating to an episodic style of fiction dealing with the adventures of a roguish hero
words.example: The novel follows a picaresque hero through eighteenth-century Europe.

words.definition: relating to the common people; unrefined
words.example: The senator sneered at what he called plebeian tastes.

words.definition: a strong verbal or written attack on a particular opinion
words.example: The pamphlet is a fierce polemic against censorship.

words.definition: lacking imagination; dull and ordinary
words.example: His prosaic description failed to capture the beauty of the scene.

words.definition: the place of origin or earliest known history of something
words.example: Experts questioned the painting's provenance before the auction.

words.definition: exceedingly idealistic; unrealistic and impractical
words.example: His quixotic scheme to end world hunger single-handedly impressed no investors.

words.definition: having an obstinately uncooperative attitude
words.example: The recalcitrant committee refused to approve any changes.

words.definition: little known; abstruse and obscure
words.example: The lecture delved into recondite corners of medieval law.

words.definition: strongly reminiscent or suggestive of something
words.example: The old house was redolent of a bygone era.

words.definition: to make an abstract idea concrete or real
words.example: The monument reifies the nation's collective memory of the war.

words.definition: grimly mocking or cynical
words.example: He gave a sardonic smile at the absurd suggestion.

words.definition: relating to tailoring, clothes, or style of dress
words.example: The actor was known as much for his sartorial elegance as his talent.

words.definition: showing dedication and diligence
words.example: Her sedulous research took nearly a decade to complete.

words.definition: the view that the self is all that can be known to exist
words.example: The novel's narrator descends into a kind of solipsism, doubting all else is real.

words.definition: the use of clever but false arguments, especially to deceive
words.example: Critics accused the debater of sophistry rather than genuine argument.

words.definition: the amalgamation of different religions, cultures, or schools of thought
words.example: The temple's architecture reflects a striking religious syncretism.

words.definition: saying the same thing twice in different words, needlessly
words.example: 'Free gift' is a common tautology in advertising.

words.definition: relating to the explanation of phenomena by their purpose rather than their cause
words.example: The essay offers a teleological reading of evolutionary theory.

words.definition: a lament for the dead; a song of mourning
words.example: The symphony's final movement is a threnody for the fallen.

words.definition: mentally or physically inactive; lethargic
words.example: The heat left the whole town feeling torpid by midafternoon.

words.definition: shaking or quivering slightly, often from fear or weakness
words.example: Her tremulous voice betrayed how nervous she was.

words.definition: eager or quick to argue or fight; aggressively defiant
words.example: The truculent customer refused to accept any apology.

words.definition: the fact of appearing everywhere at once
words.example: The ubiquity of smartphones has transformed daily life.

words.definition: offence or annoyance
words.example: She took umbrage at his careless remark.

words.definition: excessively flattering or ingratiating; oily in manner
words.example: The unctuous salesman's compliments made her suspicious.

words.definition: the appearance of being true or real
words.example: The novel's historical detail lends it great verisimilitude.

words.definition: the language or dialect spoken by ordinary people in a region
words.example: The poet wrote in the local vernacular rather than in formal English.

words.definition: remaining as a diminished remnant of something that was once functional
words.example: The appendix is often described as a vestigial organ.

words.definition: experienced through the feelings or actions of another person
words.example: She felt vicarious excitement watching her sister accept the award.

words.definition: filled with bitter criticism or malice
words.example: The critic's vitriolic review devastated the young playwright.

words.definition: vehement or clamorous in expressing one's views
words.example: A vociferous group of protesters gathered outside the courthouse.

words.definition: having or showing a feeling of vague or regretful longing
words.example: He gave a wistful glance at his childhood home as they drove past.

words.definition: the defining spirit or mood of a particular period in history
words.example: The film perfectly captured the zeitgeist of the 1960s.

words.definition: concerned with beauty or the appreciation of beauty
words.example: The architect prioritised aesthetic harmony over pure function.

words.definition: a story, poem, or picture that can be interpreted to reveal a hidden meaning
words.example: Animal Farm is a famous allegory of the Russian Revolution.

words.definition: the highest point in the development of something; elevation to divine status
words.example: The final symphony is the apotheosis of his career.

words.definition: a very typical example of a certain person or thing; an original model
words.example: The wise old mentor is a common archetype in mythology.

words.definition: a self-taught person
words.example: Largely an autodidact, she taught herself six languages from library books.

words.definition: a modulation or inflection in the rhythm and sound of language
words.example: The preacher's rising cadence held the congregation spellbound.

words.definition: accepted as a standard or authoritative part of a body of work
words.example: The novel is now considered part of the canonical literature of the period.

words.definition: the process of releasing strong or repressed emotions
words.example: Watching the tragedy provided a strange sense of catharsis.

words.definition: existing only as an illusion; wildly fanciful
words.example: His chimerical business plan collapsed within a month.

words.definition: a method of argument based on examining and reconciling contradictory ideas
words.example: The philosophy course centres on Hegelian dialectic.

words.definition: a sudden and striking realisation
words.example: She had an epiphany about her career while walking home.

words.definition: an idealising or excessively flattering biography
words.example: The documentary was criticised as hagiography rather than honest history.

words.definition: something reused or altered but still bearing visible traces of its earlier form
words.example: The city itself is a palimpsest of Roman, medieval, and modern architecture.

words.definition: an artistic work that imitates the style of another work, artist, or period
words.example: The film is a loving pastiche of 1980s adventure movies.

words.definition: excessively proud of oneself; boastful
words.example: The vainglorious general took credit for a victory he barely influenced.
